coredata.dc:title: Construction of carbon dots having aggregation-induced emission and intramolecular charge transfer properties
coredata.dc:creator: Kou, Xinyue
Wang, Yucai
Mei, Qian
Dong, Wen-Fei
Li, Li
coredata.dc:description: Aggregation-induced emission–fluorescent carbon dots (AIE-CDs) have applications in both academia and industry. In this study, triphenylamine-based CDs (T-CDs) prepared in acetic acid showing both intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) and aggregation-induced emission (AIE) were designed and synthesized. Intriguingly, the T-CDs fluoresced with different colors in solvents of different polarities: for example, in acetic acid, they showed blue fluorescence. In contrast, in the presence of water, aggregation and precipitation occurred because of the hydrophobic nature of the compound. With time, the increasing aggregation resulted in a loss in intramolecular rotational degrees of freedom, resulting in bright red emissions as a result of ICT and AIE. Interestingly, the T-CDs could be used for intracellular imaging and the lysosome-specific targeting of live cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation-induced emission
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Intramolecular charge transfer
Intracellular imaging
Lysosome targeting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cytotoxicity and intracellular imaging of T-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: T-CDs formation, appearance, and fluorescence. (a) Preparation of T-CDs and the formation of aggregates. (b) Photographs of T-CD dual fluorescence emissions.

Characterization of T-CDs. (a) TEM image of the T-CDs. Scale bar is 20 nm. (b) HR-TEM image of the T-CDs (inset: fast Fourier transform (FFT) diffraction pattern of the carbon lattice). Scale bars are 5 nm in main image and 2 nm in inset. (c) XRD patterns of the T-CDs.
Deconvoluted (a) C 1s, (b) O 1s, and (c) N 1s XPS spectra and (d) FT-IR spectrum.
Emission properties of T-CDs. (a) Fluorescence emission spectra of as-prepared T-CDs and (b) T-CD powder at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Photographs of the T-CDs solutions under sunlight (upper frame) and under 480 nm irradiation (lower frame). (b) PL emission spectra of the T-CDs in solvents having different polarities (acetic acid, ethanol, DMF, DMSO, and water). Inset shows the enlarged region in the yellow box. (c) The proposed mechanism of fluorescence change of T-CDs from AIE.

Emission properties of T-CDs after the addition of water. (a) Photographs of the as-prepared T-CDs solution from 0 to 48 h after the addition of water under sunlight (the top row) and 480 nm irradiation (the down row). (b) FL emission spectra of the as-prepared T-CDs solution under 380 nm. (c) FL emission spectra of the as-prepared T-CDs solution under 480 nm. (d, e) change in the fluorescence intensities at 434 and 633 nm with respect to time after the addition of water.

(a) Bright-field image and confocal microscopy images of HeLa cells costained with T-CDs and DAPI. (b) T-CDs and (c) DAPI emissions. (d) Merged confocal and bright-field microscopy images showing the localization of T-CDs and DAPI in the cytoplasm and nuclei, respectively. Scale bars are approximately 25 μm.

(a) Bright-field image and confocal microscopy images of HeLa cells costained with T-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Green. (b) T-CDs and (c) Lyso-Tracker Green fluorescence. (d) Merged confocal and bright-field microscopy images showing the colocalization of T-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Green in the lysosomes. Scale bars are approximately 25 μm.
Schematic of the preparation, fluorescence emissions, and applications of T-CDs.

head.ce:title: Construction of carbon dots having aggregation-induced emission and intramolecular charge transfer properties

Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aggregation-induced emission–fluorescent carbon dots (AIE-CDs) have applications in both academia and industry. In this study, triphenylamine-based CDs (T-CDs) prepared in acetic acid showing both intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) and aggregation-induced emission (AIE) were designed and synthesized. Intriguingly, the T-CDs fluoresced with different colors in solvents of different polarities: for example, in acetic acid, they showed blue fluorescence. In contrast, in the presence of water, aggregation and precipitation occurred because of the hydrophobic nature of the compound. With time, the increasing aggregation resulted in a loss in intramolecular rotational degrees of freedom, resulting in bright red emissions as a result of ICT and AIE. Interestingly, the T-CDs could be used for intracellular imaging and the lysosome-specific targeting of live cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a relatively novel member of the extended family of carbon nanomaterials having a small size, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, tunable surface properties, and significant photobleaching resistance [1,2]. As a result of these excellent properties, CDs have been widely used in various fields, including biosensing [3–5], photocatalysis [6,7], bioimaging [8,9], drug delivery [10,11] and anti-counterfeiting technologies [1,12]. However, as a result of resonance energy transfer (ET) or direct π-π stacking, the tuning of the emission energy bandgap and control of aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) are challenging [13–16] and this has limited the development and application of CDs. To mitigate the ACQ effect, chemical, physical, and engineering approaches can be used to prevent the aggregation of the luminophores [17]. However, to date, these efforts have been only marginally successful.

In 2001, Tang and coworkers reported an intriguing phenomenon named aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [18]. AIE fluorophores are non-emissive when well-dissolved in solution but become highly emissive in the aggregated state because of the restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) that deactivates radiative decay channels [19–22]. More recently, AIE dyes have been used to prepare highly fluorescent nanoparticles, and these have been widely used in cellular imaging [23–26].

To date, although AIE-CDs have been reported, few rational strategies have been suggested to generate and enhance their fluorescence in the aggregated state. However, achieving tunable emission and multicolor AIE-CDs is challenging. In particular, the fluorescence emissions of AIE-CDs typically occur at the CDs surface, and the dual fluorescence emission from the CDs surface is attractive for the construction of intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) donor–acceptor (D–A) conjugates [1,27,28]. Such ICT phenomena are common in fluorescent dyes based on D–A conjugates and molecular rotors [29,30], but are not common in CDs.

In this study, we used triphenylamine (TPA) and acetic acid as starting material to prepare CDs because of its unique non-planar propeller-like conformation. Exploiting this, CDs having ICT and AIE properties were prepared, denoted T-CDs. Crucially, these materials do not suffer from ACQ and show tunable multicolor emissions in solvents having different polarities. Further, the T-CDs show blue and red dual fluorescence emissions in solution because of the active intramolecular rotations of the CDs, which results in a slight blue fluorescence. However, in the presence of water, aggregation occurs, and the intramolecular rotations of the T-CDs are impeded, resulting in bright red fluorescence. Using control experiments, we have isolated the luminescence mechanism of the T-CDs. In addition, we have explored the potential of the T-CDs for biological imaging and lysosome targeting in living HeLa cells (Scheme 1 ).

The characterization of the morphologies of the T-CDs was carried out using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM; JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffractometry (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Bruker (Germany) diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained using a Kratos Axis Ultra system with a monochromatic Al K α X-ray source. Fourier transform-infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a Fourier FT-IR spectrometer (PerkinElmer Inc., Waltham, MA, USA). All photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained with a F-4600 spectrophotometer (Hitachi Japan). Cell imaging was achieved using a Nikon A1+ Confocal laser fluorescence microscope.

Triphenylamine, tetraphenylethylene (TPE), acetic acid, and ethanol were purchased from Adamas Ltd., Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), dimethyl formamide (DMF), and anhydrous citric acid (CA) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. 2-(4-Amidinophenyl)-6-indolecarbamidine (DAPI) dihydrochloride, 2-(4-iodophenyl)-3-(4-nitrophenyl)-5-(2,4-disulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium sodium salt (WST-1), 4% paraformaldehyde, and Lyso-Tracker Green were obtained from the Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Double-distilled water was used throughout all experiments.

TPA (0.245 g) was dissolved in acetic acid (40 mL) and sonicated for 30 min. The mixture was transferred to an 80-mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and solvothermal treatment was carried out at 180 °C for 12 h. After the T-CDs solution had cooled to room temperature, deionized water was added in a 1:1 ratio, and this resulted in the immediate precipitation of a powder. The resulting suspension was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min after sonication for 5 min, and this process was repeated three times to remove the solvent and any remaining starting material. Finally, the solid powder was dried at 60 °C in vacuum drying oven.

The standard WST-1 method with HeLa cells was used to explore the cytotoxicity of T-CDs. The HeLa cells were seeded into a 96-well plate and cultured for 24 h. Subsequently, the original medium was discarded, and 100 μL of fresh medium containing different concentrations of T-CDs (0–500 μg/mL) was added. The cells were then further cultured for 24 h. Finally, 20 μL of WST-1 was added, and the cells were incubated again for 40 min. Then, cell viability was assessed using a microplate reader at a wavelength of 450 nm. The HeLa cells were cultured in medium supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS) in a humidity incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 °C.

For cell imaging, the HeLa cells were incubated with 2 mL of culture medium containing 50 μg/mL T-CDs solution for 120 min. Then, the cells were washed three times with phosphate buffered saline (PBS). After 1 h, counterstaining with a DAPI paraformaldehyde solution was carried out for 15 min, and the cells were observed by confocal microscopy.

For the lysosomal colocalization study (that is, to confirm the specific targeting of T-CDs to lysosomes in cells), the HeLa cells were incubated with 50 μg/mL T-CDs solution for 120 min and then further incubated with a commercial lysosome-specific probe (Lyso-Tracker Green, 100 nM) for 30 min.

Using TPA as the key component, T-CDs were prepared in acetic acid solution by a one-pot solvothermal method and simple purification strategy (Fig. 1 a). The unique structure of TPA and the choice of solvent are crucial for the formation of these T-CDs. After solvothermal synthesis, the addition of water in a 1:1 ratio resulted in an instant change from a transparent dark-blue solution to a blue–white turbid liquid, which, after 2 h, became light green. After the addition of water, fluorescence under 480 nm visible light was observed. As time progressed, the non-fluorescent solution started to fluoresce yellow and, finally, red. After purification, separation by centrifugation, and drying, a green powder that fluoresced red under 480 nm blue light irradiation was obtained (Fig. 1b).

The as-prepared T-CDs were characterized using HR-TEM, XRD, XPS, and FT-IR measurements. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the as-prepared T-CDs were uniformly dispersed and showed no signs of aggregation. Further, the HR-TEM image of the T-CDs shows clear lattice fringes of about 0.21 nm, consistent with the (223) planes of graphitic carbon and the XRD data (Fig. 2b). Specifically, the XRD pattern of the T-CDs contains a significant peak near 20° consistent with the spacing on the TEM image and indicating the presence of a graphene-like structure in the T-CDs (Fig. 2c) [31].

The full-scan XPS spectrum of the T-CDs contain peaks at binding energies of 285.08, 401.58, and 532.08 eV, which correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. S1). Peak analysis revealed the ratios of C, N, and O to be 56.85%, 8.48%, and 34.67%, respectively. Deconvolution of the high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3 a) revealed peaks at 284.78, 286.48, and 288.88 eV, which correspond to C–C/C=C, C–O/C–N, and C=O/C=N bonds, respectively [32]. The deconvolution of the N1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) revealed peaks at 401.78, 400.38, and 398.76 eV, which correspond to graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N, and pyridinic-N, respectively [33]. Further, a peak corresponding to N– H bonds in the samples suggests that amide bonds were formed during synthesis [34]. The deconvoluted O1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) contains two doublets corresponding to C=O (533.27 eV) and C–O (531.95 eV) [35]. Next, the functional groups in the T-CDs were analyzed using FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 3d), and the spectra of the T-CDs and TPA are clearly different (Fig. S2), indicating the formation of a new structure after the carbonization of TPA. Crucially, the spectrum of the T-CDs contains a characteristic peak at 3441 cm-1, which is consistent with amino and oxygen-containing functional groups. In addition, a band appeared at about 1640 cm−1, which can be attributed to amide carbonyl, and at 1586 and 1491 cm−1, which could correspond to the stretching vibrations of C=C bonds. Finally, the peak at 1278 cm−1 was assigned to C–NH bonds. Overall, the functional groups identified from the XPS spectra are consistent with those indicated by the FT-IR spectra, suggesting that the produced CDs contained nitrogen-containing functional groups.

The UV–Vis absorption, FL excitation, and emission of the as-prepared T-CDs solution and powder were examined to evaluate their optical properties (Fig. S3). In both solution and the solid state, the T-CDs exhibited different optical properties. In solution, with increase in the excitation wavelength, the intensity of the fluorescence signal increased, and the T-CDs exhibited a single emission peak at 434 nm under 380 nm excitation (Fig. 4 a). However, there was no fluorescence when the excitation wavelength exceeded 400 nm. Fig. 4b shows the fluorescence spectra of T-CDs powder obtained at different excitation wavelengths. When the excitation wavelength was lower than 380 nm, with the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission peak signal at 434 nm is gradually enhanced, whereas, beyond 380 nm, the intensity of the fluorescence signal increased gradually. The T-CDs powder exhibited the optimal emission at 633 nm under 480 nm excitation, showing red fluorescence. As the excitation wavelength continued to increase, new emission peaks appeared at 684–825 nm. Therefore, the fluorescence emissions are excitation-wavelength-dependent, and the emissions undergo a continuous redshift, resulting in the appearance of a new shoulder peak (Fig. S4). To further investigate the optical properties, total luminescence spectrum was obtained, as shown in Fig. S5. It can clearly observe the fluorescence emission of T-CDs in the dispersed and aggregated states. As shown in Fig. S6, the Commission Internationale de l′ Eclairage (CIE) of T-CDs coordinates (0.1648, 0.1131) and (0.6175, 0.3787) also confirmed these phenomena.

To study the effect of solvents on fluorescence, the T-CDs were dissolved in different solvents (acetic acid, ethanol, DMF, DMSO, and water) at a concentration of 1 mg/mL, and the fluorescence color (Fig. 5 a) and emission spectra (Fig. 5b) were recorded. The polarity of acetic acid is ε = 6.2, and the fluorescence color of the T-CDs dissolved in acetic acid is used as a reference. Dissolution in ethanol (ε = 4.3) did not result in a change in the fluorescence color, and there was almost no visible fluorescence under 480 nm irradiation. In DMF (ε = 6.4) and DMSO (ε = 7.2) solutions, the main fluorescence peak was observed at 558 nm, that is, orange. In water (ε = 10.2), the intensity of the fluorescence signal was greatly enhanced, and the sample showed bright red fluorescence, having a maximum at 633 nm. These changes in the fluorescence properties could be a result of the D–A architecture in the T-CDs. In other word, after hydrothermal reaction, carbon dots with core shell structure are generated. The carbonized core is a graphene stacking-like rigid structure that emits bright blue light (Fig. 5c), and the shell is a surface loaded TPA flexible molecule. Due to the good solubility of TPA in acetic acid, its three benzene ring structures have sufficient rotational freedom, so the fluorescence efficiency of TPA in T-CDs is very low. When the solvent is replaced with water, its low solubility leads to the restriction of the intramolecular rotation (RIR) of TPA on the T-CDs surface, which makes the three benzene ring structures of TPA rotate to the same plane and form the AIE effect with adjacent molecules. In this way, the conjugated structure of TPA will bring red fluorescence, and additionally, RIR and AIE will greatly improve the fluorescence efficiency. Therefore, when T-CDs are in aqueous solution, the carbonized core acts as the Donor part and the TPA on the surface acts as the Acceptor part, thus ICT occurs in T-CDs (Fig. 5c). Specifically, with the increase of solvent polarity, TPA on T-CDs surface is more likely to form AIE, so ICT characteristics become stronger, and the fluorescence signal intensity increases along with a redshift [36].

Photographs of the T-CDs solutions under solar and 480 nm blue light are shown in Fig. 6 a. The photographs were taken 48 h after water had been added to the solution in a 1:1 ratio (as described in Section 2, the original solvent was acetic acid). On the addition of water, a powder precipitated out of solution, and the originally clear and transparent solution became an opaque cloudy suspension. Further, the color changed from blue to green over time. Under 480 nm visible light irradiation, the solution exhibited colorful fluorescence, ranging from light yellow to bright red.

Fig. 6b and c shows the changes in the emission spectra of the T-CDs solutions having different fluorescence colors with respect to time (0–48 h) after the addition of water. With increase in precipitation time up to 8 h, the fluorescence intensity at 434 nm slowly increased; after 8 h, the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply. In addition to this, after 6 h, a fluorescence peak in the red region (633 nm) appeared, and the intensity of this peak continued to increase until reaching its maximum after 48 h. The intensities of the signals at 434 and 633 nm with respect to time after the addition of water are plotted in Fig. 6d and e, respectively.
To explore the luminescence mechanism of the T-CDs, three experiments were carried out based on the substitution or addition of materials (see Supporting Information).
In the first experiment, TPE was used to prepare the CDs, and the corresponding CDs are denoted A-CDs. The solvent was the same as that use for the preparation of the T-CDs.

The prepared A-CDs solution was a white, cloudy liquid, as shown in Fig. S7, and emitted yellow fluorescence under 480 nm blue light. Eight hours after the addition of either 10% or 50% water, there was no change in the fluorescence color, which remained yellow. Therefore, the replacement of TPA with a hydrophobic material does not result in the AIE effect, and there is no redshift in the fluorescence with time.

In the second experiment, TPA was used, but the solvent was replaced with ethanol. The other reaction conditions remained unchanged. The products of these experiments are denoted B-CDs. Fig. S8 shows that, 8 h after the addition of water to the prepared solution of B-CDs, a solid was precipitated that showed bright yellow fluorescence, but there was no red fluorescence.

In our third experiment, CA was used as the carbon source, and the products of this experiment are denoted C-CDs. The color of the C-CDs solution in sunlight is similar to that of the T-CDs. However, under 480 nm blue light irradiation, the C-CDs solution was black without any fluorescence (Fig. S9).

The results of these experiments suggest that the conformational flexibility of the non-planar phenyl groups of TPA plays a significant role in the fluorescence properties of the T-CDs after the reaction of TPA with acetic acid. On the basis of these findings, a possible luminescence mechanism is proposed. Because TPA has a propeller-like conformation, the molecules at the T-CDs surfaces adopt an extremely twisted conformation and have many rotational degrees of freedom in solution. These intramolecular rotations result in the loss of exciton energy, yielding slight blue fluorescence under 380 nm UV light excitation. However, in the presence of sufficient water, precipitation occurs because of the hydrophobicity of the T-CDs. Further, with time, aggregation occurs, which impedes the intramolecular rotations; moreover, π-π stacking interactions between aromatic rings are formed as a result of the molecular conformation, resulting in greatly enhanced emissions [37,38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and intracellular imaging of T-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Next, experiments were performed to determine the cytotoxicity of the T-CDs. For this, the standard WST-1 assay was used to assess the viability of HeLa cells following the addition of T-CDs. Quantitative evaluation showed that >90% of the cells treated with T-CDs at a high concentration of 100 μg/mL remained alive after 24 h of incubation (Fig. S10), indicating good biocompatibility and suggesting potential applications in biological imaging.

To test the possibility of using the T-CDs for imaging, HeLa cells were co-incubated with T-CDs for 120 min and the commercial nuclear stain DAPI. Fig. 7 shows that the costained HeLa cells exhibited blue fluorescence in the nuclei, consistent with the color of DAPI, and emitted the red fluorescence of T-CDs in the peripheral cytoplasm. Crucially, the merged bright field and fluorescence image shows that the incubation of HeLa cells with T-CDs for 120 min leads to strong cytoplasm-specific fluorescence. Thus, as a result of their biocompatibility, T-CDs were sequestered in the cytoplasm, suggesting applications of T-CDs in intracellular imaging.

Next, we investigated the potential for using T-CDs for lysosome targeting in living cells. To achieve this, the colocalization of T-CDs and the commercial lysosome-specific dye Lyso-Tracker Green was carried out. As shown in Fig. 8 , visual inspection shows that the characteristic red fluorescence of the T-CDs overlaps the green fluorescence of Lyso-Tracker Green. Next, we used the “Color 2” program to obtained statistical information concerning the overlap of the fluorescence data, and the scatter plot corresponding to the data in Fig. 8 is shown in Fig. S11 [39]. Briefly, Fig. S11 also shows data points evenly distributed on both sides of the diagonal line, and the Pearson's correlation coefficient for the overlap of the fluorescence data is good: 0.93. Overall, the overlap shown in the merged image and the high colocalization coefficient suggest that the T-CDs are specifically distributed in lysosomes in living cells. Currently, there are few lysosome-specific CDs for cell imaging, suggesting the promise of the T-CDs for this purpose. This phenomenon can be attributed to the large amount of the amino group of T-CDs. Since the T-CDs enter into cells through the endocytosis process of lysosomes, the acidotropic effect of the amino group induces the T-CDs to largely accumulate in the acidic lysosomes [40].

We used TPA, which has a non-planar propeller-like conformation, to synthesize T-CDs in a simple solvothermal method in glacial acetic acid (also one of the important raw materials), followed by full characterization of their morphological, chemical, and optical properties. In different solvents, the T-CDs show a bathochromic shift as a result of ICT. Briefly, the T-CDs emit blue light under UV irradiation when dissolved but show red-shifted, intense emissions upon aggregate formation. Owing to the combined ICT and AIE, the T-CDs emit red light intensely. Further, the T-CDs showed good biocompatibility with HeLa cells, as well as selective localization in the cytoplasm, specifically lysosomes, suggesting their potential for applications in intracellular imaging and lysosome targeting.

Xinyue Kou: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editingEditing. Yucai Wang: Resources, Supervision, Investigation, Review, Validation, Funding acquisition. Qian Mei: Methodology, Investigation. Wen-Fei Dong: Resources, Supervision, Validation, Formal analysis. Li Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grand No. 2020YFC2004600), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 91959112, 81902166, 62027825, and 82172077), the Science Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. 2020SYHZ0041), the Instrument Developing Project of Chinese Academy of Science (YJKYYQ20200038), the Youth Innovation Promotion Association of CAS (2022326), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2019683), and the Science and Technology Department of Jinan City (2018GXRC016).

The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2023.111092.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrophobic carbon dots with blue dispersed emission and red aggregation-induced emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Yang, Y. Liu, Z. Guo, B. Lei, J. Zhuang, X. Zhang, Z. Liu, C. Hu, Hydrophobic carbon dots with blue dispersed emission and red aggregation-induced emission, Nature Communications 10 (2019) 1-11.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation and biomedical applications of multicolor carbon dots: recent advances and future challenges

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Huo, P.G. Karmaker, Y. Liu, B. Zhao, X. Yang, Preparation and Biomedical Applications of Multicolor Carbon Dots: Recent Advances and Future Challenges, Particle & Particle Systems Characterization 37(4) (2020) 1900489.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carboxylic carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for DNA detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.H. Loo, Z. Sofer, D. Bousa, P. Ulbrich, A. Bonanni, M. Pumera, Carboxylic Carbon Quantum Dots as a Fluorescent Sensing Platform for DNA Detection, Acs Applied Materials & Interfaces 8(3) (2016) 1951-1957.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coke-derived graphene quantum dots as fluorescence nanoquencher in DNA detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.T. Yew, A.H. Loo, Z. Sofer, K. Klimova, M. Pumera, Coke-derived graphene quantum dots as fluorescence nanoquencher in DNA detection, Applied Materials Today 7 (2017) 138-143.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red/orange dual-emissive carbon dots for pH sensing and cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhang, R. Su, J. Zhong, L. Fei, W. Cai, Q. Guan, W. Li, N. Li, Y. Chen, L. Cai, Q. Xu, Red/orange dual-emissive carbon dots for pH sensing and cell imaging, Nano Research 12(4) (2019) 815-821.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Good steel used in the blade: well-tailored type-I photosensitizers with aggregation-induced emission characteristics for precise nuclear targeting photodynamic therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Kang, Z. Zhang, W. Xu, H. Wen, W. Zhu, Q. Wu, H. Wu, J. Gong, Z. Wang, D. Wang, B.Z. Tang, Good Steel Used in the Blade: Well-Tailored Type-I Photosensitizers with Aggregation-Induced Emission Characteristics for Precise Nuclear Targeting Photodynamic Therapy, Advanced Science 8(14) (2021) 2100524.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-free efficient photocatalyst for stable visible water splitting via a two-electron pathway
